pattern: A short string of length L can only hold so many distinct binary substrings, so checking each value's binary form for membership — and bailing early when n is too large to possibly fit — settles the question fast.
hints: For each integer from one to n, you just need to know whether its binary form appears somewhere inside s.
A string of length L contains at most a limited number of distinct substrings, so for large n the answer is forced to false before you even check — use that to bound the work.
Iterate i from one to n, convert i to binary, and test substring membership; return false on the first miss, true if all are present.

edge_inputs.description: small string that covers one through three
same string fails to cover four
string lacks the representation of two
string containing all of one two three
n far too large for a short string
longer string covering up to five

stress.description: moderately long random binary string with a small n so checking stays bounded
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    L = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('01') for _ in range(L))
    n = rng.randint(1, 200)
    return [s, n]
stress.note: n kept small so the reference enumerates quickly
